questions.question: Let A be an array of 3k numbers consisting of a sequence of 0's followed by a sequence of 1's. The problem is to find the smallest index i such that A[i] = 1 by probing the minimum number of locations in A. The worst case number of probes performed by an optimal algorithm is ____________.
questions.to_find: Worst case number of probes by an optimal algorithm to find the first 1 in an array of 3^k elements (sequence of 0s then 1s)

understand.plain: We have an array of 3^k elements that looks like [0, 0, ..., 0, 1, 1, ..., 1]. We need to find the position where 1s begin. The optimal strategy is like ternary search — probe the middle element, and depending on the result narrow the search. We want the minimum number of probes in the worst case.

keywords.explain: A divide-and-conquer search strategy that splits the array into three equal parts at each step, reducing the problem size by a factor of 3 each time.
keywords.example: For an array of 27 elements, probe positions 9 and 18 to determine which third the boundary lies in.

keywords.explain: The maximum number of array accesses needed over all possible positions of the 0-to-1 transition boundary.
keywords.example: If 3^k = 27, the worst case is probing 3 times (log_3(27) + 1 = 4), but optimally we can do it in k+1 probes.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='500' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='20' y='15' class='sub ink'>Array of 3^k = 27 (k=3) elements: 0s followed by 1s</text><rect x='10' y='20' width='185' height='40' style='fill:#e8e4d4;stroke:#B4B2A9;stroke-width:1;'/><text x='100' y='45' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>0 0 0 ... 0</text><rect x='195' y='20' width='315' height='40' style='fill:#d4f0e4;stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:1;'/><text x='360' y='45' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>1 1 1 ... 1</text><text x='196' y='75' class='sub' style='fill:#D85A30;' text-anchor='middle'>^ boundary</text><line x1='196' y1='60' x2='196' y2='75' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><text x='20' y='110' class='sub ink'>Probe 1: mid = 3^k / 3 = 3^(k-1)</text><text x='20' y='130' class='sub ink'>Probe 2: narrow to 1/3 of remaining ...</text><text x='20' y='150' class='sub ink'>After k probes: interval of size 1; probe k+1 to confirm. Total = k+1 probes.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array of 3^k elements with 0s on the left and 1s on the right. Ternary search divides the array into thirds at each probe, reducing the size from 3^k to 3^(k-1) each step. After k steps the interval is size 1, and one more probe confirms, giving k+1 total probes.

given.aim: Find the minimum worst-case number of probes to locate the first 1 in a 0...0 1...1 array of size 3^k.

terms.meaning: Total number of elements in the array.
terms.connects: Array size determines search depth in ternary search.

terms.meaning: The index i such that A[i]=1 and A[i-1]=0 (or i=0).
terms.connects: This is exactly what we are searching for.

terms.meaning: Ternary search: probe at 1/3 and 2/3 of current range each round, or equivalently probe at 1/3 first (since value is monotone: 0s then 1s).
terms.example: For n=9: probe index 3. If A[3]=0, boundary is in [4..9]; if A[3]=1, probe 1 to check further.
terms.connects: Since array is monotone (0s then 1s), one probe per step suffices to reduce search space by factor 3.

given.plan: Use ternary search: probe A[n/3]. If 0, the boundary is in the upper 2/3; if 1, it may be in the lower 1/3. Each probe reduces the problem size by a factor of 3. Starting from 3^k, after k probes size is 1; we need one more probe to confirm. Total = k+1.

steps.title: Model as ternary search on monotone array
steps.apply: Since the array is monotone (all 0s then all 1s), probing at position n/3 = 3^(k-1) tells us whether the boundary is in the first third or not. If A[3^(k-1)] = 0, boundary is in the last 2/3; probe at 2*3^(k-1) to further narrow to 1/3. Either way, one probe reduces the active range by a factor of 3. This gives recurrence T(3^k) = T(3^(k-1)) + 1.
steps.note: The array being sorted (monotone) means binary-style search works, but ternary is better here due to the special structure of size 3^k.

steps.title: Solve the recurrence
steps.apply: Expanding: T(3^k) = T(3^(k-1)) + 1 = T(3^(k-2)) + 2 = ... = T(3^0) + k = T(1) + k. T(1) = 1 (one probe to confirm the single element). Therefore T(3^k) = k + 1.
steps.note: k = log_3(n), so the answer is log_3(n) + 1. For n = 3^k, this equals k+1.

solution.result: The worst case number of probes performed by an optimal algorithm is k + 1.

questions.formula_note: Ternary search on a monotone 0-1 array of size 3^k: each probe reduces the search space by a factor of 3, giving a recurrence T(3^k) = T(3^(k-1)) + 1 with solution k+1.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements regarding Breadth First Search (BFS) and Depth First Search (DFS) on an undirected simple graph G is/are TRUE?

A. A DFS tree of G is a Shortest Path tree of G.
B. Every non-tree edge of G with respect to a DFS tree is a forward/back edge.
C. If (u, v) is a non-tree edge of G with respect to a BFS tree, then the BFS levels of u and v differ by at most 1.
D. Both BFS and DFS can be used to find the connected components of G.
questions.to_find: Which statements about BFS and DFS on an undirected simple graph are TRUE

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of properties of BFS and DFS trees in undirected graphs. We need to evaluate each statement about what types of edges appear, path lengths, and what problems these algorithms can solve. BFS always finds shortest paths (in unweighted graphs), while DFS explores deeply before backtracking.

keywords.explain: A spanning tree of a graph built by BFS, where tree edges connect nodes level by level. Non-tree edges in undirected graphs connect nodes on the same or adjacent BFS levels.
keywords.example: In a graph where BFS visits A->B->C->D, the BFS tree has edges A-B, A-C, B-D. An edge C-D would be a non-tree edge between adjacent levels.

keywords.explain: A spanning tree built by DFS. In undirected graphs, all non-tree edges are back edges (connecting a descendant to an ancestor); there are no cross edges or forward edges.
keywords.example: In a DFS of A->B->C where C connects back to A, the edge C-A is a back edge.

keywords.explain: A tree rooted at the source where the path from the root to any node in the tree is a shortest path in the original graph.
keywords.example: BFS from a source gives a shortest path tree for unweighted graphs; DFS generally does not.

understand.visual_alt: Consider a triangle graph A-B-C-A. BFS from A: tree edges A-B, A-C; non-tree edge B-C is between adjacent BFS levels (level 1). DFS from A: tree edges A-B, B-C; back edge C-A connects C to its ancestor. This illustrates the properties of BFS and DFS non-tree edges.

given.aim: Determine which properties of BFS and DFS trees on undirected simple graphs are correct.

terms.meaning: A graph with no directed edges, no self-loops, and no multiple edges between the same pair of vertices.
terms.example: A road network between cities with bidirectional roads.
terms.connects: The graph type affects which edges can appear in BFS/DFS trees.

terms.meaning: An edge of the original graph that is not part of the BFS/DFS spanning tree.
terms.example: In DFS of a triangle, two edges form the tree and one is a non-tree (back) edge.
terms.connects: The classification of non-tree edges (back/cross/forward) characterizes BFS and DFS.

terms.meaning: The distance (in hops) of a vertex from the BFS source, i.e., its layer in the BFS tree.
terms.example: Source is at level 0; its direct neighbors are at level 1; their unvisited neighbors at level 2.
terms.connects: BFS level equals shortest path distance from source in unweighted graph.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement using known properties: (A) DFS tree is generally NOT a shortest path tree; (B) in undirected DFS all non-tree edges are back edges (no forward/cross edges); (C) BFS non-tree edges connect vertices differing by at most 1 in BFS level; (D) both BFS and DFS identify connected components.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A: DFS tree is a shortest path tree
steps.apply: FALSE. DFS explores deeply and does not guarantee shortest paths. For example, in a graph with vertices {A, B, C} and edges A-B, B-C, A-C: DFS from A might visit A->B->C (path length 2 from A to C via tree), while the direct edge A-C (length 1) is the shortest path but may not be in the DFS tree. Only BFS guarantees shortest paths in unweighted undirected graphs.
steps.note: A DFS tree gives spanning tree paths that follow DFS exploration order, not shortest distances.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement B: DFS non-tree edges are forward/back edges
steps.apply: FALSE (partially). In an undirected graph, DFS non-tree edges are ONLY back edges (connecting a vertex to its ancestor). There are no forward edges or cross edges in undirected DFS. The statement says 'forward/back edge' which includes forward edges — forward edges don't exist in undirected DFS trees. So the statement is imprecise/false: the correct characterization is 'back edges only'.
steps.note: In directed graphs, DFS can produce forward and cross edges, but in UNDIRECTED graphs, only back edges occur as non-tree edges.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement C: BFS non-tree edge endpoints differ by at most 1 in BFS level
steps.apply: TRUE. In BFS on an undirected graph, if (u,v) is a non-tree edge, then u and v must be on the same level or adjacent levels. Proof: suppose u is at level l. BFS visits all level-l nodes before level-(l+1) nodes. If v were at level l+2 or beyond when the edge (u,v) is first discovered, then v would have been discovered earlier through u (contradiction). So |level(u) - level(v)| <= 1.
steps.note: This is a fundamental property of BFS in undirected graphs: non-tree edges are either 'same-level' or 'adjacent-level' edges.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement D: Both BFS and DFS find connected components
steps.apply: TRUE. Both BFS and DFS can find all connected components of an undirected graph by running from each unvisited vertex. All vertices reachable from a source in one BFS/DFS run form one connected component. Repeating for unvisited vertices finds all components.
steps.note: Connected component detection is a standard application of both BFS and DFS with O(V+E) time complexity.

solution.result: Statements C and D are TRUE. Statements A and B are FALSE.

questions.formula_note: Key properties: BFS gives shortest paths in unweighted graphs; undirected DFS has only back edges as non-tree edges; BFS non-tree edges connect vertices at levels differing by at most 1; both BFS and DFS identify connected components.

questions.question:
Fill in the blanks in the following template of an algorithm to compute all pairs shortest path lengths in a directed graph G with n vertices and adjacency matrix A. A[i][j] equals the weight w(i,j) if there is an edge in G from i to j, and infinity otherwise.

Your aim in filling in the blanks is to ensure that the algorithm is correct.

INITIALIZATION:
For i = 1 to n do
  For j = 1 to n do
    D[i][j] = A[i][j];

ALGORITHM:
For k = 1 to n do
  For i = 1 to n do
    For j = 1 to n do
      __(P)__;

Which one of the following CORRECTLY fills the blank (P)?

A. D[i][j] = min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j])
B. D[i][j] = min(D[i][j], D[i][k] * D[k][j])
C. D[i][j] = D[i][j] + min(D[i][k], D[k][j])
D. D[i][j] = min(D[i][k], D[j][k] + D[k][j])
questions.to_find: The correct update step for Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest path algorithm

understand.plain: This is asking us to complete the Floyd-Warshall algorithm for finding shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted directed graph. The algorithm works by progressively allowing paths through intermediate vertices 1, 2, ..., n. The key update rule relaxes the path from i to j by checking whether going through vertex k gives a shorter path.

keywords.explain: A dynamic programming algorithm for all-pairs shortest paths. D[i][j] after the k-th outer iteration gives the shortest path from i to j using only vertices {1, ..., k} as intermediates.
keywords.example: If D[1][3] = 10 and D[1][2] + D[2][3] = 7, then using vertex 2 as intermediate gives a shorter path: D[1][3] = 7.

keywords.explain: The operation of updating a path estimate if a shorter route is found through an intermediate vertex.
keywords.example: D[i][j] = min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j]) — check if going i->k->j is shorter than current best i->j.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='80' cy='80' r='28' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='85' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>i</text><circle cx='220' cy='40' r='28' class='hi'/><text x='220' y='45' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>k</text><circle cx='360' cy='80' r='28' class='bg'/><text x='360' y='85' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>j</text><line x1='108' y1='70' x2='192' y2='48' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='248' y1='52' x2='332' y2='70' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='108' y1='85' x2='332' y2='85' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,4'/><text x='160' y='32' class='sub' style='fill:#1D9E75;'>D[i][k]</text><text x='270' y='75' class='sub' style='fill:#1D9E75;'>D[k][j]</text><text x='200' y='105' class='sub' style='fill:#D85A30;'>D[i][j] direct</text><text x='10' y='145' class='sub ink'>Update: D[i][j] = min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j])</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three vertices i, k, j. Green path goes i->k->j with cost D[i][k]+D[k][j]. Red dashed shows direct path D[i][j]. Floyd-Warshall picks the minimum of these two options.

given.aim: Identify the correct relaxation step for the Floyd-Warshall all-pairs shortest path algorithm.

terms.meaning: Current best-known shortest path distance from vertex i to vertex j.
terms.example: Initially D[i][j] = A[i][j] (direct edge weight or infinity).
terms.connects: Updated during the algorithm to reflect paths through intermediate vertices.

terms.meaning: The vertex being added as a potential relay in the current outer loop iteration.
terms.example: When k=2, we check if going through vertex 2 shortens any path i to j.
terms.connects: The outer loop over k is the DP dimension: after k iterations, D[i][j] = shortest path using vertices {1..k} only.

terms.meaning: Takes the smaller of two values: keep old estimate or accept new (shorter) path.
terms.connects: Ensures D[i][j] always holds the shortest path found so far.

given.plan: The Floyd-Warshall recurrence is D_k[i][j] = min(D_{k-1}[i][j], D_{k-1}[i][k] + D_{k-1}[k][j]). In the iterative version this becomes D[i][j] = min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j]). Check each option against this formula.

steps.title: Recall Floyd-Warshall recurrence
steps.apply: The DP state D_k[i][j] = shortest path from i to j using intermediate vertices from {1, ..., k}. To go from k-1 to k: either don't use k (keep D_{k-1}[i][j]) or go through k (D_{k-1}[i][k] + D_{k-1}[k][j]). In-place update works because D[i][k] and D[k][j] are not changed when k is fixed as the outer loop variable.
steps.note: The + operator combines path segments; min selects the shorter option — this is the hallmark of shortest path relaxation.

steps.title: Match to given options
steps.apply: Option A: D[i][j] = min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j]) — matches the Floyd-Warshall formula exactly. Option B uses multiplication instead of addition (wrong — path lengths add, don't multiply). Option C: D[i][j] = D[i][j] + min(...) always increases D[i][j] (wrong). Option D has wrong indices: D[i][k], D[j][k]+D[k][j] doesn't make sense semantically.
steps.note: Option A is the classic Floyd-Warshall relaxation step, standard for all-pairs shortest paths in weighted directed graphs.

solution.result: Option A: D[i][j] = min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j]) is the correct update step.

questions.formula_note: Floyd-Warshall algorithm: dynamic programming on intermediate vertices. Recurrence D[i][j] = min(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j]) captures the choice of routing through vertex k or not.

questions.question:
Let G = (V, E) be an undirected graph with a subgraph G_1 = (V_1, E_1). Weights are assigned to edges of G as follows:

w(e) = 0 if e in E_1
       1 otherwise

A single-source shortest path algorithm is executed on the weighted graph (G, w) with an arbitrary vertex v_1 of V_1 as the source. Which of the following can always be inferred from the path costs computed?

A. The number of edges in the shortest paths from v_1 to all vertices of G.
B. G_1 is connected.
C. V_1 forms a clique in G.
D. G_1 is a tree.
questions.to_find: What can always be inferred from shortest path costs when edges in subgraph G1 have weight 0 and others have weight 1

understand.plain: We assign weight 0 to all edges inside the subgraph G1 and weight 1 to all other edges. Then we run a shortest path algorithm from a vertex v1 in V1. The path cost from v1 to any vertex in V1 equals 0 if and only if that vertex is reachable from v1 using only zero-weight (G1) edges — meaning G1 is connected. This lets us determine connectivity of G1.

keywords.explain: Edges with cost 0 assigned to all edges within subgraph G1, meaning traveling along them is free.
keywords.example: If G1 is a triangle {A,B,C}, all edges A-B, B-C, A-C have weight 0.

keywords.explain: If all vertices of V1 are reachable from v1 with path cost 0, it means there are paths using only G1 edges — proving G1 is connected.
keywords.example: If d(v1, u) = 0 for all u in V1, then G1 is connected. If d(v1, u) > 0 for some u in V1, G1 is disconnected.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='80' cy='90' r='25' class='hi'/><text x='80' y='95' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>v1</text><circle cx='200' cy='50' r='25' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='55' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>u</text><circle cx='200' cy='140' r='25' class='hi'/><text x='200' y='145' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>w</text><circle cx='330' cy='90' r='25' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='95' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>x</text><line x1='105' y1='78' x2='175' y2='60' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='3'/><text x='125' y='60' class='sub' style='fill:#1D9E75;font-weight:600;'>w=0</text><line x1='105' y1='103' x2='175' y2='130' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='3'/><text x='115' y='130' class='sub' style='fill:#1D9E75;font-weight:600;'>w=0</text><line x1='200' y1='75' x2='200' y2='115' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='225' y1='90' x2='305' y2='90' stroke='#5F5E5A' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='5,4'/><text x='255' y='83' class='sub mut'>w=1</text><text x='10' y='170' class='sub ink'>d(v1,u)=0, d(v1,w)=0 => G1 connected. d(v1,x)=1 => x not in V1 or G1 disconnected.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph showing subgraph G1 nodes v1, u, w connected by weight-0 edges (green). External vertex x is connected by a weight-1 edge. Shortest path costs of 0 from v1 to all V1 vertices imply G1 is connected.

given.aim: Determine what property of G1 can always be inferred from shortest path costs in the 0-1 weighted graph.

terms.term: w(e) = 0 for e in E1, 1 otherwise
terms.meaning: Edge weight assignment: free travel within G1, cost 1 to cross outside G1.
terms.example: Edge between two G1 vertices costs 0; edge to a non-G1 vertex costs 1.
terms.connects: Shortest path costs directly measure how many steps outside G1 are needed.

terms.meaning: We start the shortest path from a vertex inside G1.
terms.example: Run Dijkstra from v1; any vertex reachable via only G1 edges gets distance 0.
terms.connects: Allows us to test connectivity of G1 from this source.

terms.term: Path cost = 0 for u in V1
terms.meaning: If d(v1, u) = 0 for all u in V1, then all V1 vertices are reachable from v1 using only G1 edges.
terms.example: In a connected G1, all pairs of V1 vertices are reachable within G1 (zero cost paths).
terms.connects: Equivalently, d(v1, u) = 0 for all u in V1 if and only if G1 is connected.

given.plan: Check each option: (A) Path cost != number of edges; (B) d(v1,u)=0 for all u in V1 iff G1 is connected; (C) Clique is a stronger structural property not inferrable from costs alone; (D) Tree requires specific edge count and no cycles, not inferrable. Option B is correct.

steps.title: Analyze what shortest path costs represent
steps.formula_raw: d(v1, u) = minimum sum of edge weights on path from v1 to u
steps.apply: Since E1-edges have weight 0 and others weight 1, the shortest path cost d(v1, u) equals the minimum number of non-E1 edges needed to reach u from v1. In particular, d(v1, u) = 0 iff u is reachable from v1 using only E1-edges (i.e., only within G1). This directly measures G1-reachability.
steps.note: This is a classic trick: assign 0 to 'free' edges and 1 to 'paid' edges to test connectivity using shortest paths (or even BFS since costs are 0/1).

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: Option A: Path cost is not the number of edges (costs are 0/1, so the number of edges can be much larger than the cost). FALSE. Option B: d(v1, u) = 0 for all u in V1 if and only if G1 is connected (all V1 vertices reachable from v1 within G1). If any u in V1 has d(v1, u) > 0, G1 is disconnected. This is always inferable. TRUE. Option C: Clique means every pair in V1 is directly connected by an edge. Cost alone cannot tell us if V1 forms a clique vs. just a connected subgraph. FALSE. Option D: A tree means G1 is connected and acyclic with |V1|-1 edges. We cannot determine acyclicity or edge count from path costs alone. FALSE.
steps.note: Connectivity of G1 is the exact property captured by zero-cost reachability from v1 in the 0-1 weight assignment.

solution.result: Option B: G1 is connected — this is the property that can always be inferred from the shortest path costs.

questions.formula_note: 0-1 weight assignment (w=0 for subgraph edges, w=1 otherwise): shortest path cost from source in G1 equals 0 for a vertex u iff u is reachable within G1, directly testing connectivity of G1.
